title: Graphs
description: Graph problems are interview favorites — from connected components to shortest paths. Master the adjacency list representation with Map<number, number[]>, DFS, BFS, and component counting in TypeScript.

sections.title: Map-based adjacency list
sections.explanation: The standard interview graph representation is an adjacency list using `Map<number, number[]>`. Each key is a node, and its value is an array of neighbors. For undirected graphs, add the edge in both directions. Building the graph from an edge list is the first step of almost every graph problem.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph with 5 nodes (0-4) and edges [[0,1],[0,2],[1,3],[3,4]]. Print each node's neighbor list as "node: [neighbors]".

validation.message: Each node should list its neighbors from the undirected edges
Use Map adjacency list with bidirectional edge insertion

exercise.hint: For edge [u,v], push v into u's list and u into v's list. This makes it undirected.

sections.title: Graph DFS
sections.explanation: DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Use a `Set<number>` to track visited nodes and prevent cycles. The recursive pattern: mark as visited, process the node, then recurse on unvisited neighbors. DFS is used for pathfinding, cycle detection, and topological sorting.

exercise.prompt: Implement DFS traversal starting from node 0. Build a graph with edges [[0,1],[0,2],[1,3],[2,4]]. Print the DFS visit order as comma-separated values.

validation.message: Use a visited Set to prevent revisiting nodes

exercise.hint: visited.add(node) marks it seen. visited.has(neighbor) checks if already visited before recursing.

sections.title: Graph BFS
sections.explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level using a queue. It finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. Use a `Set<number>` for visited nodes and a `number[]` queue (shift from front, push to back). Mark nodes as visited when enqueuing, not when dequeuing — this prevents duplicates.

exercise.prompt: Implement BFS that also tracks distance from the start node. Build a graph with edges [[0,1],[0,2],[1,3],[3,4]]. Print each node and its distance from node 0.

validation.message: Use BFS with distance tracking via Map

exercise.hint: shift() dequeues. Use dist.has() instead of a separate visited set — if a node has a distance, it's been visited. Each neighbor's distance is parent's distance + 1.

sections.title: Connected components
sections.explanation: Counting connected components means finding how many separate clusters of nodes exist. Iterate all nodes; for each unvisited node, run a DFS/BFS to mark all reachable nodes, then increment the component count. This is O(V + E) and appears in island-counting and friend-group problems.

exercise.prompt: Count the number of connected components in a graph with 7 nodes and edges [[0,1],[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]]. Print the count.

validation.message: Use DFS to explore each component and count total components

exercise.hint: Call dfs(neighbor) to recurse. Check !visited.has(i) to find unvisited nodes. Increment count after each new DFS call.

sections.title: Cycle detection
sections.explanation: To detect cycles in an undirected graph, track the parent of each node during DFS. If you visit a neighbor that's already visited and it's not your parent, you've found a cycle. For directed graphs, use three states: unvisited, in-progress, and completed.

exercise.prompt: Implement cycle detection for an undirected graph. Test with two graphs: one with a cycle (edges [[0,1],[1,2],[2,0]]) and one without ([[0,1],[1,2],[2,3]]). Print true/false for each.

validation.message: First graph has a cycle (0-1-2-0), second is a simple chain
Track parent to distinguish back-edges from tree-edges

exercise.hint: Pass the current node as the parent when recursing: dfs(neighbor, node). A visited neighbor that isn't the parent means a cycle exists.
